column1: #deck:Corebook::All Categories::Intermediate::Neuro::Spine::Anatomy::Spinal Osseous Structures, Ligaments, and Muscles::Craniocervical Junction

column15: What structures define the craniocervical junction?
column16: The craniocervical junction is C1, C2, and their articulation with the skull base. The normal review should include the occiput, atlas, axis, their articulations, and the supporting ligaments.
column21:
🧭 Craniocervical Junction

The craniocervical junction comprises C1, C2, the skull-base articulation, and supporting ligaments. The article emphasizes normal bony components, atlantooccipital and atlantoaxial joints, ligament order, biomechanics, and radiographic alignment measurements.

🔑 Core Anatomy
  • CCJ includes the occiput, atlas, axis, their articulations, and ligaments.
  • Occipital condyles are paired oval inferior prominences of the lateral exoccipital occipital bone.
  • C1 has anterior and posterior arches, paired lateral masses, large transverse processes, and no body.
  • C2 has a large body, superiorly projecting dens, anteriorly positioned superior facets, and posterior inferior facets with an elongated pars interarticularis.
📍 Joint Map
  • Atlantooccipital joints pair convex occipital condyles with concave superior C1 facets.
  • Median atlantoaxial joint is the pivot between the dens and the ring formed by the anterior C1 arch and transverse ligament.
  • Lateral atlantoaxial joints pair concave inferior C1 facets with convex superior C2 facets.
🧾 Ligament Stack
  • Anterior-to-posterior order: anterior atlantooccipital membrane, odontoid ligaments, cruciate ligament, tectorial membrane, posterior atlantooccipital membrane.
  • Apical ligament runs from dens tip to basion; alar ligaments run from lateral dens tip to anteromedial occipital condyles.
  • Transverse ligament runs between lateral C1 masses behind the dens; the craniocaudal cruciate component extends to the foramen magnum and C2.
📊 Alignment Measurements
  • C1-2 interspinous space should be 10 mm or less.
  • ADI is less than 3 mm in adults and less than 5 mm in children in flexion.
  • Wackenheim line follows the posterior clivus; the posterior odontoid tip should lie immediately inferior and this relationship does not change with flexion-extension.
  • Welcher basal angle is normal below 140 degrees, with an average of 132 degrees.
  • Chamberlain and McGregor lines assess odontoid elevation relative to skull-base reference lines.
  • Clivus canal angle is about 180 degrees in extension, 150 degrees in flexion, and below 150 degrees is abnormal.
⚠️ Pearls / Pitfalls
  • Pediatric pseudosubluxation can be physiologic at C2-3 and C3-4 up to age 8; anterior displacement of C2 on C3 may measure up to 4 mm.
  • Posterior cervical line helps separate physiologic C2-3 displacement from abnormal alignment: the anterior C2 spinous process should be within 2 mm of the line.
  • Frontal review should confirm C1 and C2 lateral mass alignment, while recognizing that overlapping lateral masses can be a normal pediatric variant.
⭐ Super Summary
  • First build the CCJ map: occipital condyles, C1 ring/lateral masses, C2 dens/body, then joints and ligament stack.
  • Then assess lateral and frontal alignment using source-stated measurement lines and pediatric variant rules.

column15: What bony map should anchor first-pass craniocervical junction review?
column16: Use a skull-base-to-axis map: occipital condyles -> C1 anterior and posterior arches with lateral masses -> C2 body and dens. Establish this bony framework before judging joints, ligaments, or alignment lines.

column15: What are the normal occipital condyles at the craniocervical junction?
column16: They are paired, oval-shaped inferior prominences from the lateral exoccipital portion of the occipital bone. Their articular facets project laterally.

column15: What basic C1 features distinguish the atlas from a typical cervical vertebra?
column16: C1 is the atlas. It is composed of anterior and posterior arches and has no vertebral body.

column15: Which C1 structures carry the articular facets and transverse foramina?
column16: The paired lateral masses carry the superior and inferior articular facets. C1 also has large transverse processes containing the transverse foramina.

column15: What are the defining body and dens features of C2 at the craniocervical junction?
column16: C2 is the axis. It has a large body and a superiorly projecting odontoid process.

column15: How do the C2 articular facets differ superiorly versus inferiorly?
column16: The superior articulating facet surface is convex, directed laterally, and positioned relatively anteriorly. The inferior articular process and facet are typical of lower cervical vertebrae and lie more posteriorly, with an elongated pars interarticularis.

column15: How are the normal atlantooccipital joint surfaces arranged?
column16: The inferior articular facet of each occipital condyle is oval and convex and projects laterally. The superior C1 articular facet is oval, concave anteroposteriorly, and projects medially.

column15: What forms the normal median atlantoaxial pivot joint?
column16: It is a pivot-type joint between the dens and the ring formed by the anterior arch of C1 plus the transverse ligament of C1.

column15: Where are the synovial cavities in the median atlantoaxial joint?
column16: The article describes synovial cavities between the transverse ligament and odontoid and between the atlas and odontoid articulations.

column15: How are the normal lateral atlantoaxial joint surfaces arranged?

🧭 Craniocervical Junction

The craniocervical junction comprises C1, C2, the skull-base articulation, and supporting ligaments. The article emphasizes normal bony components, atlantooccipital and atlantoaxial joints, ligament order, biomechanics, and radiographic alignment measurements.

🔑 Core Anatomy
  • CCJ includes the occiput, atlas, axis, their articulations, and ligaments.
  • Occipital condyles are paired oval inferior prominences of the lateral exoccipital occipital bone.
  • C1 has anterior and posterior arches, paired lateral masses, large transverse processes, and no body.
  • C2 has a large body, superiorly projecting dens, anteriorly positioned superior facets, and posterior inferior facets with an elongated pars interarticularis.
📍 Joint Map
  • Atlantooccipital joints pair convex occipital condyles with concave superior C1 facets.
  • Median atlantoaxial joint is the pivot between the dens and the ring formed by the anterior C1 arch and transverse ligament.
  • Lateral atlantoaxial joints pair concave inferior C1 facets with convex superior C2 facets.
🧾 Ligament Stack

  • Apical ligament runs from dens tip to basion; alar ligaments run from lateral dens tip to anteromedial occipital condyles.
  • Transverse ligament runs between lateral C1 masses behind the dens; the craniocaudal cruciate component extends to the foramen magnum and C2.

  • Posterior cervical line helps separate physiologic C2-3 displacement from abnormal alignment: the anterior C2 spinous process should be within 2 mm of the line.
  • Frontal review should confirm C1 and C2 lateral mass alignment, while recognizing that overlapping lateral masses can be a normal pediatric variant.
⭐ Super Summary
  • First build the CCJ map: occipital condyles, C1 ring/lateral masses, C2 dens/body, then joints and ligament stack.
  • Then assess lateral and frontal alignment using source-stated measurement lines and pediatric variant rules.

column15: How is the posterior cervical line used when assessing pediatric C2-3 alignment?
column16: Draw a line from the anterior aspects of the C1 through C3 spinous processes. The anterior C2 spinous process should lie within 2 mm of this line.
column21:
🧭 Craniocervical Junction

The craniocervical junction comprises C1, C2, the skull-base articulation, and supporting ligaments. The article emphasizes normal bony components, atlantooccipital and atlantoaxial joints, ligament order, biomechanics, and radiographic alignment measurements.

🔑 Core Anatomy
  • CCJ includes the occiput, atlas, axis, their articulations, and ligaments.
  • Occipital condyles are paired oval inferior prominences of the lateral exoccipital occipital bone.
  • C1 has anterior and posterior arches, paired lateral masses, large transverse processes, and no body.
  • C2 has a large body, superiorly projecting dens, anteriorly positioned superior facets, and posterior inferior facets with an elongated pars interarticularis.
📍 Joint Map
  • Atlantooccipital joints pair convex occipital condyles with concave superior C1 facets.
  • Median atlantoaxial joint is the pivot between the dens and the ring formed by the anterior C1 arch and transverse ligament.
  • Lateral atlantoaxial joints pair concave inferior C1 facets with convex superior C2 facets.
🧾 Ligament Stack
  • Anterior-to-posterior order: anterior atlantooccipital membrane, odontoid ligaments, cruciate ligament, tectorial membrane, posterior atlantooccipital membrane.
  • Apical ligament runs from dens tip to basion; alar ligaments run from lateral dens tip to anteromedial occipital condyles.
  • Transverse ligament runs between lateral C1 masses behind the dens; the craniocaudal cruciate component extends to the foramen magnum and C2.
📊 Alignment Measurements
  • C1-2 interspinous space should be 10 mm or less.
  • ADI is less than 3 mm in adults and less than 5 mm in children in flexion.
  • Wackenheim line follows the posterior clivus; the posterior odontoid tip should lie immediately inferior and this relationship does not change with flexion-extension.
  • Welcher basal angle is normal below 140 degrees, with an average of 132 degrees.
  • Chamberlain and McGregor lines assess odontoid elevation relative to skull-base reference lines.
  • Clivus canal angle is about 180 degrees in extension, 150 degrees in flexion, and below 150 degrees is abnormal.

  • Posterior cervical line helps separate physiologic C2-3 displacement from abnormal alignment: the anterior C2 spinous process should be within 2 mm of the line.
  • Frontal review should confirm C1 and C2 lateral mass alignment, while recognizing that overlapping lateral masses can be a normal pediatric variant.
⭐ Super Summary
  • First build the CCJ map: occipital condyles, C1 ring/lateral masses, C2 dens/body, then joints and ligament stack.
  • Then assess lateral and frontal alignment using source-stated measurement lines and pediatric variant rules.

column15: What is the normal atlantooccipital joint angle on frontal craniocervical junction assessment?
column16: The angle is formed at the junction of lines traversing the atlantooccipital joints. Normal is 125 to 130 degrees; less than 124 degrees may reflect condyle hypoplasia.

column15: What frontal relationship should be checked between the lateral masses of C1 and C2?
column16: The lateral masses of C1 and C2 should align on frontal assessment. Overlapping lateral masses can be a normal variant in children.

column9: Use the posterior cervical line: the anterior C2 spinous process should be within 2 mm of the line from the anterior C1-3 spinous processes.
column13: What pediatric alignment pitfall can mimic abnormal subluxation near the craniocervical junction?
column14: Physiologic anterior displacement may be seen up to age 8: about 40% at C2-3 and 14% at C3-4. Anterior displacement of C2 on C3 can be up to 4 mm.

column11: Why is the median atlantoaxial joint the main rotational joint of the cervical spine?
column12: It is a pivot-type joint: the dens rotates within the ring formed by the anterior arch of C1 and the transverse ligament. The article states that the atlantoaxial joint accounts for about 50% of cervical rotation.

column11: Why is the atlantooccipital joint the main flexion-extension joint at the craniocervical junction?
column12: The joint pairs the oval convex occipital condyle with the oval, anteroposteriorly concave superior articular facet of C1. The article states that this joint accounts for about 50% of cervical flexion-extension and allows limited lateral motion.
